elements.sent-2411-001: We present an approximation algorithm for the Prize-col\-lect\-ing Ordered Traveling Salesman Problem (\PCOTSP), which simultaneously generalizes the Prize-collecting TSP and the Ordered TSP.
elements.sent-2411-002: The Prize-collecting TSP is well-studied and has a long history, with the current best approximation factor slightly below $1.6$, shown by Blauth, Klein and Nägele [IPCO 2024].
elements.sent-2411-003: The best approximation ratio for Ordered TSP is $\frac{3 {2 +\frac{1 {e}$, presented by B\"{o hm, Friggstad, M\"{o mke, Spoerhase [SODA 2025] and Armbruster, Mnich, N\"{a gele [Approx 2024].}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The former also present a factor 2.2131 approximation algorithm for \kTSP.
elements.sent-2411-005: By carefully tuning the techniques of the latest results on the aforementioned problems and leveraging the unique properties of our problem, we present a \appfac-approximation algorithm for \PCOTSP.
elements.sent-2411-006: A key idea in our result is to first sample a set of trees, and then probabilistically pick up some vertices, while using the pruning ideas of Blauth, Klein, N\"{a gele [IPCO 2024] on other vertices to get cheaper parity correction; the sampling probability and the penalty paid by the LP playing a crucial part in both cases.}
elements.sent-2411-007: A straightforward adaptation of the aforementioned pruning ideas would only give minuscule improvements over standard parity correction methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Instead, we use the specific characteristics of our problem together with properties gained from running a simple combinatorial algorithm to bring the approximation factor below 2.1.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our techniques extend to Prize-collecting \kpath TSP, building on results from B\"{o hm, Friggstad, M\"{o mke, Spoerhase [SODA 2025], leading to a \appfackpath-approximation.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The metric traveling salesman problem (TSP), which asks for a shortest closed tour in a metric space $(V,c), \ c: V\times V \to \mathbb{Q _+$ on an $n$ element vertex set $V$ visiting each vertex exactly once \footnote{Note that the problem can alternatively be defined as having a weighted graph as input, and seeking to find a shortest closed walk (or shortest Eulerian multi-subgraph) that visits each vertex at least once.}}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Our Contribution and Overview of Techniques
elements.sent-2411-032: We study \OTSP and \kTSP in the prize-collecting setting, which can also be viewed as generalizations of \PCTSP.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the Prize-collecting Ordered Traveling Salesman Problem (PC\-OTSP) we are given a metric weighted complete graph with penalties for vertices $G=(V,E), \ c\colon E\to \mathbb{R _+, \pi:V\to \mathbb{R _+$, together with $k$ terminals $O=\{o_1, \ldots, o_k\}$, and the objective is to find a tour $C$, minimizing $c(C) + \sum_{v\not\in V(C) \pi(v)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In the Prize-collecting \kpath Traveling Salesman Problem (\PCkTSP), we are given a metric weighted complete graph with vertex penalties $G=(V,E), \ c\colon E\to \mathbb{R _+, \pi\colon V\to \mathbb{R _+$, together with a set of $k$ terminal pairs $\mathcal{T =\{(s_1,t_1), \ldots, (s_k,t_k)\}$, and the objective is to find $k$ paths $P_i, 1\le i\le k$, such that $P_i$ is a path with endpoints $s_i, t_i$, minimizing $\sum_{i=1 ^k c(P_i) + \sum_{v\not\in (\cup V(P_i)) \pi(v)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the remainder of this section we provide an overview of our algorithms and techniques for \PCOTSP and \PCkTSP.
elements.sent-2411-038: The details of the algorithms and the proofs of correctness are covered in the following sections In \cref{sec:pcotsp , we specify the LP relaxation (OLP) of \PCOTSP.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Our algorithm first solves the LP, and then it samples a tree $T_i$ for each of the $k$ parts of the LP solution, in the manner of Lemma 2 in \cite{blauth-klein-naegele-2024 , and Theorem 4 in \cite{boehm-friggstad-moemke-2025 , which are both based on a result of Bang Jensen, Frank and Jackson~\cite{bangjensen-frank-jackson-1995 and Post and Swamy~\cite{post-swamy-2015}\footnote{Post and Swamy showed that the Bang Jensen, Frank, Jackson decomposition can be computed in polynomial time.. (see \cref{lem:bang-jensen ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The expected cost of each tree is at most the corresponding part of the LP solution, and its expected coverage is at least that of the corresponding $y$ values.
elements.sent-2411-041: Now a key idea in our algorithm is to even out the penalty ratios paid by the algorithm, by either reducing the penalties paid by some vertices through connecting them up with the $T_i$'s (which inevitably increases the connectivity cost), or utilizing the spare penalty ratios of other vertices to bring down the cost of parity correction.
elements.sent-2411-042: More precisely, we use the pruning idea from \cite{blauth-klein-naegele-2024 to probabilistically prune away portions of the resultant structure with low connectivity.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This allows us to assign weights to the edges of the tree, which, when combined with a suitable multiple of the LP solution, gives a point in the $Q$-join polytope, where $Q$ is the set of odd degree vertices of the current structure.
elements.sent-2411-044: This allows for cheaper parity correction, because the tree edges with higher assigned weights, which are associated with lower value cuts, are pruned with higher probability, and thus the expected cost incurred by them remains low.
elements.sent-2411-045: The prominent difference between our problem and the standard \PCTSP, which necessitates finding new ideas for pruning, is the sampling probability for vertices.
elements.sent-2411-046: In \PCTSP, the sampling probability for each vertex $v$ is equal to $y_v$, hence the expected penalty ratio for $v$ is one, and intuitively, there is lots of spare penalty for $v$ to utilize for pruning.
elements.sent-2411-047: But in \PCOTSP, the sampling probability of a vertex $v$ is lower (bounded from below by $1-e^{-y_v}$); hence there is little spare penalty ratio left (and for fewer vertices) to utilize for pruning.
elements.sent-2411-048: Thus, in order to get a nontrivial improvement from pruning we need new ideas.
elements.sent-2411-049: This can be partly achieved by combining our algorithm with a version of the classical algorithm for \OTSP~\cite{boeckenhauer-hromkovic-kneis-2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: That algorithm simply combines the cycle on terminals with a $\frac{3 {2}$-approximation for TSP on the remaining vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-051: If the length of the cycle on terminals is low, this algorithm gives a good overall approximation ratio.
elements.sent-2411-052: If the length of the cycle is large, this fact can be utilized to improve the analysis for parity correction, because we can show that there is no need to assign (nonzero) weights to the cycle edges.
elements.sent-2411-053: We believe that this view of evening out the penalty ratio of vertices vis-a-vis the optimal LP solution is a very useful conceptual tool for approaching prize collecting versions of TSP or other combinatorial optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-054: In the case of \PCOTSP, it is partly realized through the new idea of probabilistically including in the solution those vertices which currently have a too high penalty ratio (based on the desired approximation factor).
elements.sent-2411-055: Squeezing out the slack penalty ratio (again, based on the target approximation factor), is achieved through pruning.
elements.sent-2411-056: Here, the specific structure of the \OTSP, and the partially constructed solution proves useful; we can show that if the current cycle through the terminals is small, combining the cycle on terminals with the best \PCTSP approximation for the rest of vertices gives a good factor, while a large cycle improves the cost of pruning.
elements.sent-2411-057: In the case of \kTSP, B\"{o hm, Friggstad, M\"{o mke, and Spoerhase \cite{boehm-friggstad-moemke-2025 propose two algorithms, and show that a careful combination of them leads to a good approximation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The first one, which doubles all edges except those on $s_i,t_i$ paths (and is good when the sum of $s_i,t_i$ distances is large) can be adapted to our setting by probabilistically picking up vertices with high penalty ratio, as opposed to picking up all remaining vertices in \cite{boehm-friggstad-moemke-2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: We replace their second algorithm, which finds a minimum length forest in which each terminal appears in exactly one of the components and then adds direct $s_i,t_i$ edges with an algorithm that uses \cref{lem:bang-jensen to sample a tree from the related \PCTSP, and then adds direct $s_i,t_i$ edges.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{The Prize-collecting Ordered TSP Algorithm \label{alg:pcotsp}} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Statex Input: An \PCOTSP instance \State Compute an optimal solution $(x^\ast, y^\ast)$ of OLP \State Split off vertices $v \in V$ with $y^\ast_v \leq \theta$ \label{line:splitting-off} \State Sample $k$ trees $T_1, \ldots T_k$ \State Sample a pruning threshold $\gamma \sim D_\gamma$ \State Prune each $T_i$ based on $\gamma$ to get $T'_i$. Let $T'=\bigcup_{i=1}^k T'_i$ \State Sample a pickup threshold $\sigma \sim D_\sigma$ \State Pick up all unsampled vertices with $y_v \geq \sigma$ to obtain $T''$ \State Add a shortest $\text{odd}(T'')$-$\text{join}$ $J$ to $T''$ to get $H$ \State \Return an ordered tour obtained by shortcutting $H$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-130: For a fixed LP solution $(x, y)$, a tree $T$, and a threshold $\gamma \in [0, 1]$, we define $\text{core (T, \gamma)$ as the inclusion-wise minimal subtree of $T$ that spans all vertices in $V(T) \cap V_\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Algorithmically, $\text{core (T, \gamma)$ can be obtained by iteratively removing leaves $v$ with $y_v < \gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: To prune a tree $T_i$ simply means to replace it by $T_i^\prime = \text{core (T_i, \gamma)$.}
elements.sent-2411-133: We emphasize that for pruning $T_i$, we do not consider the local penalty values $y_{i, v}$, but the global values $y_{v}$.
